blocks.text: Chapter 2.
How Money Warps Judgment
The ideas in 30 seconds
Money already spent is gone forever; only future costs and benefits should drive a decision, yet the sunk cost fallacy makes us pour more resources into failing projects to justify past investment.
Losses hurt roughly twice as much as equivalent gains feel good, so we take irrational risks to avoid losses and respond far more to threats than to opportunities.
Merely owning something inflates its value in our eyes — the endowment effect — which is why sellers routinely demand far more than buyers will pay for the identical item.
People respond to incentives, not to the intentions behind them: any reward system will be optimized for literally, often producing the opposite of what its designer wanted.
Money that arrives easily — winnings, bonuses, inheritances, "free" credit — feels less real than earned money and gets gambled or squandered, even though every dollar has identical purchasing power.
Paying people for things they once did willingly can destroy their intrinsic motivation, and extra income stops buying happiness surprisingly fast because we adapt to every gain.
Sunk Costs: The Money You've Lost Is Already Gone

The sunk cost fallacy is the tendency to continue an endeavor because of what you have already invested — money, time, effort, emotion — rather than because of its future prospects. Rationally, past investment is irrelevant: it cannot be recovered no matter what you do next. Only the expected costs and benefits from this moment forward should matter.

The canonical example is the Concorde. Britain and France knew early on that the supersonic airliner would never be commercially viable, yet both governments kept funding it for decades because so much national money and prestige had already been sunk into the project. Abandoning it felt like admitting the earlier spending was a waste — so they wasted more. Economists still call irrational project escalation the "Concorde effect."

The fallacy is everywhere once you look. You sit through a dreadful film because you paid for the ticket, even though leaving would save you ninety minutes and cost you nothing extra — the ticket price is lost either way. Investors refuse to sell a plunging stock because they are "waiting to get back to what I paid," anchoring on a purchase price the market does not know or care about. Couples stay in dead relationships because of the years already invested. Companies keep doomed ad campaigns and product lines alive because killing them would mean admitting error.

What powers the fallacy is not stupidity but the desire to appear — and feel — consistent. Walking away signals that our earlier judgment was wrong, and we hate that admission more than we hate further losses. Dobelli's test is brutal in its simplicity: ignore everything you have put in, and ask only whether, starting fresh today, you would still choose this project, this stock, this relationship. If the answer is no, the only rational reason to continue does not exist — everything else is just past investment talking.
Loss Aversion: Why Losing $100 Hurts More Than Winning $100 Feels Good

Loss aversion is the empirical finding, central to Kahneman and Tversky's prospect theory, that a loss weighs roughly twice as heavily on us as a gain of the same size. Losing $100 stings about twice as intensely as finding $100 delights. We are not neutral accountants of outcomes; we are wired to defend what we have.

Evolution explains the asymmetry. For our ancestors, a single serious loss — of food, status, or safety — could be fatal, while a missed gain was merely unfortunate. Organisms that guarded fiercely against losses outlived those that chased upside, so the bias is baked in.

The consequences ripple through modern life. In negotiations and marketing, framing something as a potential loss is far more persuasive than framing it as a forgone gain: a campaign warning "you lose money by not insulating" beats one promising "you save money by insulating," even though the two statements are logically identical. Doctors get more compliance describing what patients risk losing than what they stand to gain. (How framing manipulates choices more broadly is covered in chapter 6.)

Loss aversion also makes us timid where boldness is rational and reckless where caution is rational. Investors hold losing stocks too long — selling would convert a paper loss into a real, painful one — and sell winners too early to lock in the pleasure of a gain. Employees hide mistakes and avoid ambitious projects because a visible failure damages them more than an equivalent success helps them, which is why entire organizations drift toward risk-avoidance even when shareholders would be better served by intelligent risk-taking. You cannot switch the feeling off; you can only recognize that the pain of a loss is not evidence about what the smart move is.
The Endowment Effect: Ownership Inflates Value

The endowment effect is loss aversion applied to possessions: the moment something becomes ours, we value it more than we did before we owned it — and more than any buyer will. Selling feels like a loss, so we demand compensation not just for the object but for the pain of parting with it.

Daniel Kahneman demonstrated this with coffee mugs. Students given a mug and asked to name a selling price demanded roughly twice what other students — identical except that they hadn't been given a mug — were willing to pay for one. Nothing about the mug changed; only ownership did, and it doubled the perceived value.

Dan Ariely found a far more dramatic version at Duke University, where basketball tickets are allocated by lottery after students camp out for days. Students who lost the lottery were willing to pay around $170 for a ticket; students who won demanded around $2,400 to sell the same ticket — a fourteen-fold gap between buyers and sellers of an identical good, separated only by who happened to own it.

The effect distorts markets and lives. Homeowners overprice their houses because they price in sentimental attachment the buyer does not share. Collectors cannot rationally prune their collections. It even extends to near-ownership: bidders who spend hours leading an auction start to feel the item is already theirs, which fuels overbidding — the final-price frenzy that produces the winner's curse. Companies exploit the effect deliberately with free trials and generous return policies: once the product sits in your home, giving it back registers as a loss, and most people keep it.

Dobelli's counsel: regard possessions as things the universe has temporarily lent you, not as parts of yourself. When selling, price at what a stranger would pay, not at what parting costs you emotionally.
Incentives Rule Behavior — Including in Ways You Didn't Intend

The incentive super-response tendency — Charlie Munger's term, which Dobelli adopts — says that people respond powerfully to incentives, but to the incentive as literally defined, not to the intention behind it. Design a reward system carelessly and people will game it, often producing exactly the outcome you were paying to prevent.

The classic case comes from colonial Hanoi. French administrators, plagued by rats, offered a bounty for every rat tail delivered. Citizens responded rationally: they caught rats, cut off their tails, and released the animals to breed — more rats meant more tails meant more money. The bounty subsidized rat production. Similar stories recur wherever rewards are attached to a proxy rather than the real goal, from dead-snake bounties that spawned snake farms to sales quotas that produce padded, unprofitable deals.

The tendency explains why you should be wary of anyone whose pay depends on your decision. A lawyer billing by the hour has an incentive to let the case drag; a financial adviser paid on commission has an incentive to churn your portfolio; a surgeon paid per operation has an incentive to operate. None of these people need to be dishonest — the incentive quietly bends their honest judgment. Dobelli's rule: never ask a barber whether you need a haircut. Wherever possible, negotiate fixed prices tied to results, and always ask what the person advising you stands to gain.

Incentives can also backfire by destroying motivation rather than misdirecting it — motivation crowding. When Haifa daycare centers introduced a small fine for parents who picked their children up late, late pickups increased. The fine converted a moral obligation ("don't inconvenience the teachers") into a market transaction ("late service costs a few shekels — cheap"), and when the fine was removed, the politeness never returned. Money can crowd out goodwill permanently: pay a friend for a favor and you may buy the favor but kill the friendship's logic. Small monetary incentives are often worse than none.
Easy Come, Easy Go: The House-Money Effect

The house-money effect is our tendency to treat money differently depending on how it arrived. Money we have won, been given, or received unexpectedly feels like "the house's money" rather than our own, and we take risks with it that we would never take with earned income — even though every euro is identical in purchasing power.

Casinos are built on this quirk. A gambler up $1,000 mentally files the winnings as free money and bets it recklessly; losing it "doesn't count" because it was never really his. Richard Thaler, who named the effect, showed the same pattern in experiments: people who have just won are markedly more willing to accept gambles than people playing with their own stake. Marketers exploit it too — sign-up bonuses, cashback, vouchers, and "free" credit all feel like windfalls, so we spend them faster and less carefully than salary. Lottery winners going broke and heirs squandering inheritances are the same bias at life scale: money that wasn't earned doesn't trigger the caution that earned money does.

The economist's corrective is mental accounting discipline in reverse: refuse to keep separate mental accounts at all. A bonus, a tax refund, a winning bet, and your paycheck belong in one pot, governed by one set of rules. If you wouldn't buy the speculative stock with next month's rent money, don't buy it with your bonus either. Dobelli's practical advice: when a windfall arrives, dress it in "work clothes" — transfer it to the same account as your salary and let it cool before touching it.

A final money illusion deserves a paragraph: the hedonic treadmill. We chase raises, bigger houses, and faster cars expecting lasting happiness, but we adapt to each upgrade within months and return to our emotional baseline — studies of lottery winners find their happiness reverting toward average within about three months. The treadmill's lessons: avoid negatives that resist adaptation (commuting, noise, chronic stress), expect only brief joy from purchases, and invest instead in free time, autonomy, and friendships — the few things we never fully adapt to.
In practice

Audit one ongoing commitment — a project, subscription, investment, or relationship — and ask: knowing what I know now, would I start this today? If not, exit regardless of what you've already put in.
Before selling anything you own, write down the maximum you would pay for it as a stranger; list it at that price, not at your attachment-inflated one.
For every adviser you consult this week — lawyer, banker, agent, salesperson — explicitly ask or work out how they are paid, and discount their advice in the direction of their incentive.
Replace one hourly or per-transaction arrangement with a fixed price tied to the outcome you actually want.
Route any windfall — bonus, refund, gift, winnings — into the same account as your salary and impose a 30-day cooling period before spending a cent of it.
When weighing a decision, notice whether fear of a small loss is outvoting a larger expected gain; write both numbers down and let the arithmetic, not the anticipated pain, decide.
